---
title: "Changed paragraphs only: vignette Decision logic"
output: html_document
---

## Top-level routing by input class

On the highest level, the decision logic of `visstat()` is driven by the
class of its input vectors:

Numeric responses with categorical predictors enter the central-tendency
branch.
Ordered categorical responses with categorical predictors follow the
non-parametric Wilcoxon or Kruskal--Wallis route; when both variables are
ordered and `correlation = TRUE`, Kendall rank correlation is used.
Two numeric variables enter simple linear regression unless
`correlation = TRUE`, which switches to Spearman rank correlation.
Two unordered factors enter the proportion-comparison branch.
Definitions of all implemented test statistics and association measures
are referenced in [Appendix B](#tests).

This class-based split defines which analysis family is admissible; it
is not the distinctive statistical routing step. That step starts inside
the central-tendency branch, where group-specific sample size determines
whether residual normality is tested formally before variance
homogeneity separates equal-variance from Welch-type procedures.

Unless stated otherwise, R function names refer to functions from the
`stats` package distributed with R [@R:2026].

## General linear model diagnostics shared by two branches

Although the highest-level routing is class-based, two branches share
the general linear model framework: the mean-based central-tendency
tests and the default numeric--numeric simple-regression branch.
The assumptions of this framework are visualised before the
branch-specific decision logic is described.
Their role differs by branch: in the central-tendency branch, selected
diagnostics can affect test selection; in the simple-regression branch,
the diagnostics guide model assessment but do not trigger automatic
model replacement.

Student's t-test, Fisher's ANOVA, and simple linear regression all belong
to the general linear model framework [@Thompson:2015] and thus share a
common set of assumptions: the expected value of the response is a
linear function of the predictors, the error terms are independent and
normally distributed with expectation 0, and the error variance is
constant at $\sigma^2$ (see [Appendix A](#general-linear-model)).

For the central-tendency branch with numeric responses and for the
simple-regression branch, `vis_lm_assumptions()` displays a four-panel
diagnostic graphic before the selected test or regression result.
It fits the corresponding linear model and computes internally
studentised residuals with `rstandard()`.

The diagnostic panel shows the residual distribution with a
normal-density overlay, residuals versus fitted values, and normal Q--Q
behaviour.
The fourth plot is a scale-location plot for grouped central-tendency
analyses and a standardised residuals versus leverage plot for simple
regression.

The outer title reports the displayed assumption checks and their
$p$-values in two rows.
The first row reports residual-normality checks: Shapiro--Wilk and
Anderson--Darling.
The second row reports variance checks: Brown--Forsythe Levene-type and
Bartlett for grouped central-tendency analyses, or Breusch--Pagan for
simple regression.

Not all displayed checks enter automated routing.
The following branch-specific sections describe which checks are used
for test selection.

`levene.test()` and `bp.test()` have standalone implementations in the
package to avoid dependencies on external packages (see
[Appendix B](#homo)).

## Numeric response and categorical predictor

The following figure expands the central-tendency branch.

The first split checks group size.
The diagnostic panel is generated before this split is evaluated.
When all group-specific sample sizes are greater than 50, the normality
of the sampling distribution of the group means can be assumed by the
central limit theorem [@Lumley:2002; @Rasch:2011], and the residual
normality test is bypassed to avoid rejecting negligible deviations in
large samples [@Ghasemi:2012; @Fagerland:2012; @Shatz:2024].
Otherwise, a linear model `lm(y ~ x)` (see
[Appendix A](#general-linear-model)) is fitted for the routing decision,
and the Shapiro--Wilk (SW) test is applied to internally studentised
residuals from `rstandard()` [@Cook:1982].

## Both variables numeric

When both predictor and response are numerical, `visstat()` fits and
displays a simple linear regression.
The assumption-diagnostic panel described above is displayed, but its
checks do not trigger automatic model replacement.

## Read-through check

- Hierarchy: headings now separate top-level class routing, shared general linear model
  diagnostics, and branch-specific routing.
- Logical flow: class routing is explained first; general linear model diagnostics are
  introduced only after the two relevant branches are named.
- Connecting sentences: the general linear model bridge explains why the diagnostics
  section follows the class-routing paragraph.
- Repetition: the old duplicated general linear model sentence in the central-tendency
  section is removed; graphical output is described once.
